ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Abdominal Assessment for Nurses
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Abdominal Assessment for Nurses | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN gastrointestinal review: Abdominal Assessment for Nurses — assessment, nursing priorities, red flags, bleeding and obstruction safety, infection control, Canada- and US-friendly clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Abdominal Assessment for Nurses** is core NCLEX-RN **gastrointestinal** nursing: connect **inspection, auscultation, percussion, and palpation** themes (as the stem allows) to **perfusion, bleeding, obstruction, and infection** risk. Canadian stems may use **metric** labs and interprofessional language; **first action** logic matches US boards: **airway** when vomiting blood, **two large-bore IVs** and **type & screen** themes for unstable bleed, **NPO** when surgical abdomen is suspected, then **notify** with **objective** trends.

NCLEX rewards distinguishing **new instability** from chronic symptoms and avoiding **routine tasks** ahead of **resuscitation** or **escalation**.

Cross-link [GI bleed assessment](LESSON:gi-bleed-assessment), [liver failure & hepatic encephalopathy](LESSON:liver-failure-hepatic-encephalopathy), [acute pancreatitis care](LESSON:acute-pancreatitis-nursing-care), [bowel obstruction vs ileus](LESSON:bowel-obstruction-vs-paralytic-ileus), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay IV access** in **unstable bleed**, **offer food** before **NPO** rules are cleared in acute abdomen vignettes, or **delegate** unstable reassessment to UAP. Expect **SBAR** with **quantified** vitals, **emesis/stool** description, and **orthostatic** trends when provided.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Learning objectives (exam frame):** interpret abdominal and **stool** findings; link **vitals** to **perfusion**; choose **NPO**, **IV access**, and **escalation** when red flags appear.
- **Nursing priorities:** airway and **aspiration** protection; **volume** resuscitation per orders; **infection** and **isolation** discipline; **pain** assessment without masking **surgical** catastrophe.
- **Safety / red flags:** **rigid abdomen**, **unexplained** hypotension, **maroon** stools, **coffee-ground** emesis, **confusion** with **liver** failure, **high-output** stoma.
- **Patient teaching (when stable):** diet progression, **medication** adherence, **when to return** for bleeding or fever.
- **Clinical reasoning:** compare **new** vs **baseline**; pick **assess + stabilize + notify** before teaching.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A hospitalized adult with concerns related to **Abdominal Assessment for Nurses** develops **worsening abdominal distension**, **tachycardia**, and **dizziness** when standing. You note **guarding** on light palpation per order.

**Fork:** **NPO**, **IV access**, **repeat vitals** including **orthostatics** if ordered, **prepare labs** per protocol, and **notify the provider** with **SBAR** including **stool/emesis** details—not routine hygiene first.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Inspect, listen, palpate** themes only within scope and orders—**integrate** with **vitals** and **labs**.
- **Unstable bleed** or **acute abdomen** → **NPO**, **access**, **monitor**, **notify** before teaching.
- **Synthesis:** GI nursing is often **resuscitation in disguise**—trend **perfusion** and **mentation**.

**Related:** [GI bleed assessment](LESSON:gi-bleed-assessment) · [liver failure & hepatic encephalopathy](LESSON:liver-failure-hepatic-encephalopathy) · [bowel obstruction vs ileus](LESSON:bowel-obstruction-vs-paralytic-ileus) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

preTest.question: For Abdominal Assessment for Nurses, which action best matches NCLEX-RN prioritization?
options: Finish charting before reassessing a client with hypotension and melena
Establish IV access, monitor vitals and mentation, and notify the provider with objective findings when unstable criteria are met
Offer a regular diet to rule out anxiety-related abdominal pain
Send the UAP alone to evaluate new rigid abdomen and fever
preTest.rationale: GI emergencies require immediate assessment, IV access when indicated, perfusion and bleeding surveillance, and timely provider communication before deferrable tasks.

preTest.question: A client with risk related to Abdominal Assessment for Nurses has active hematemesis and altered consciousness. What is the priority?
options: Complete discharge teaching about diet
Protect airway, position to reduce aspiration risk, call for help, and prepare for urgent interventions per orders
Ambulate the client to stimulate bowel function
Wait for the next scheduled assessment in two hours
preTest.rationale: Active upper GI bleeding with altered consciousness threatens airway and perfusion; nursing prioritizes airway protection, assistance, and urgent escalation.

postTest.question: Which finding most urgently escalates care in a GI context for Abdominal Assessment for Nurses?
options: Mild bloating after lunch in afebrile client
Rigid abdomen with fever and tachycardia after recent abdominal surgery
Request for a vegetarian meal tray
Chronic heartburn unchanged from baseline
postTest.rationale: Peritoneal signs with fever and tachycardia suggest perforation or intra-abdominal catastrophe until evaluated and require immediate escalation.

postTest.question: When is patient teaching about Abdominal Assessment for Nurses most appropriate?
options: During active hemodynamic instability from GI bleeding
After stabilization when the client is alert, oriented, and able to participate
Only after discharge without follow-up
Before any assessment to save time
postTest.rationale: Education is safest and most effective after acute threats are stabilized and the patient can engage.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Ascites and Fluid Volume in Liver Disease
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Ascites and Fluid Volume in Liver Disease | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN gastrointestinal review: Ascites and Fluid Volume in Liver Disease — assessment, nursing priorities, red flags, bleeding and obstruction safety, infection control, Canada- and US-friendly clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Ascites and Fluid Volume in Liver Disease** focuses on **hepatobiliary** pathophysiology and nursing surveillance: **ascites**, **portal hypertension**, **variceal** bleeding risk, **ammonia** and **encephalopathy** patterns, **jaundice** workup themes, and **procedure** support for **paracentesis** when ordered. You protect **airway** if altered mentation worsens, maintain **bleeding precautions**, trend **electrolytes** and **renal function**, and teach **sodium restriction**, **lactulose/rifaximin** adherence themes, and **when to seek care** for **GI bleed** or **worsening confusion**.

Pair [liver failure & hepatic encephalopathy](LESSON:liver-failure-hepatic-encephalopathy), [GI bleed assessment](LESSON:gi-bleed-assessment), [GERD & PUD bleeding clues](LESSON:gerd-pud-bleeding-clues), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay IV access** in **unstable bleed**, **offer food** before **NPO** rules are cleared in acute abdomen vignettes, or **delegate** unstable reassessment to UAP. Expect **SBAR** with **quantified** vitals, **emesis/stool** description, and **orthostatic** trends when provided. Watch for **encephalopathy** triggers (**infection**, **constipation**, **GI bleed**, **sedatives**) and **variceal** bleed precautions.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Ascites / FVE:** daily weights, **I&O**, **abdominal girth** when ordered, **sodium** restriction teaching, **spontaneous bacterial peritonitis** fever vigilance.
- **Encephalopathy:** **asterixis** themes, **medication** adherence for ammonia-lowering therapies, **avoid constipation** and **sedation** when stem cues.
- **Varices:** soft diet teaching when stable; **avoid straining** themes; recognize **acute** **hematemesis** as emergency pathway.
- **Procedures:** **paracentesis**—verify **consent**, **baseline** vitals, **post-procedure** hypotension watch, **leak** from puncture site.
- **Hepatitis:** **rest**, **nutrition**, **avoid alcohol** and hepatotoxins per teaching stems.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client with **cirrhosis** becomes **sleepier** overnight with **flapping tremor** when arms extended. **BP** is stable but **temperature** is **38.1 °C**.

**Fork:** Treat as **encephalopathy** exacerbation until evaluated—**neuro checks**, **infection** workup themes, **review** **constipation** and **GI bleed** risk, **notify provider**, and **avoid sedating** PRN stacking without orders.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Inspect, listen, palpate** themes only within scope and orders—**integrate** with **vitals** and **labs**.
- **Unstable bleed** or **acute abdomen** → **NPO**, **access**, **monitor**, **notify** before teaching.
- **Synthesis:** GI nursing is often **resuscitation in disguise**—trend **perfusion** and **mentation**.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: GI Bleed: First Actions and Priorities
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: GI Bleed: First Actions and Priorities | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN gastrointestinal review: GI Bleed: First Actions and Priorities — assessment, nursing priorities, red flags, bleeding and obstruction safety, infection control, Canada- and US-friendly clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**GI Bleed: First Actions and Priorities** centers on **GI hemorrhage** recognition and stabilization: **hypotension**, **tachycardia**, **pallor**, **orthostasis**, **altered mental status**, and **stool or emesis** clues (**melena**, **hematochezia**, **hematemesis**). NCLEX tests **NPO**, **IV access**, **labs/blood bank** preparation, **airway protection** with active **hematemesis**, **elevate HOB** when hematemesis risk, and **frequent vitals** with **orthostatic** checks when ordered.

Integrate [GI bleed assessment](LESSON:gi-bleed-assessment), [GERD & PUD bleeding clues](LESSON:gerd-pud-bleeding-clues), [liver failure & hepatic encephalopathy](LESSON:liver-failure-hepatic-encephalopathy), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay IV access** in **unstable bleed**, **offer food** before **NPO** rules are cleared in acute abdomen vignettes, or **delegate** unstable reassessment to UAP. Expect **SBAR** with **quantified** vitals, **emesis/stool** description, and **orthostatic** trends when provided. Items contrast **upper** vs **lower** source clues and **stability** thresholds for **urgent** escalation.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **First actions:** **two** large-bore lines when unstable; **type & screen**; **NPO**; **monitor** HR/BP/mentation; prepare **blood** per protocol.
- **Airway:** **hematemesis** with altered protection → **suction**, **positioning**, **call for help**.
- **Lower GI:** **hematochezia** with instability → assume **significant** loss until evaluated.
- **Medications:** avoid **NSAIDs** teaching in active **PUD** bleed stems; follow **PPI** orders.
- **Documentation:** estimate **volume** of blood, **color**, **frequency**, and **syncope**.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** An adult reports **black tarry stools** ×2 and **lightheadedness**. **BP** **92/58**, **HR** **118**, skin **cool**.

**Fork:** **Activate GI bleed pathway** themes—**two large-bore IVs**, **type & screen**, **NPO**, **continuous monitoring**, **notify provider** urgently, and **airway** equipment ready if **emesis** worsens.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Inspect, listen, palpate** themes only within scope and orders—**integrate** with **vitals** and **labs**.
- **Unstable bleed** or **acute abdomen** → **NPO**, **access**, **monitor**, **notify** before teaching.
- **Synthesis:** GI nursing is often **resuscitation in disguise**—trend **perfusion** and **mentation**.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Enteral Feeding Tubes (NG, PEG) | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN gastrointestinal review: Enteral Feeding Tubes (NG, PEG) — assessment, nursing priorities, red flags, bleeding and obstruction safety, infection control, Canada- and US-friendly clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Enteral Feeding Tubes (NG, PEG)** covers **enteral** and **parenteral** support: **NG/PEG** placement verification themes, **residual** checks when policy allows, **head-of-bed** elevation, **aspiration** prevention, **slowing or holding** feeds for **distension** or **emesis**, and **TPN** line **asepsis** with **glucose** and **electrolyte** monitoring per orders. Boards punish **continuing tube feeds** when **aspiration** risk is high or **perforation** is suspected.

Link [enteral feeding tube safety](LESSON:enteral-feeding-tube-safety), [acute pancreatitis care](LESSON:acute-pancreatitis-nursing-care), [bowel obstruction vs ileus](LESSON:bowel-obstruction-vs-paralytic-ileus), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay IV access** in **unstable bleed**, **offer food** before **NPO** rules are cleared in acute abdomen vignettes, or **delegate** unstable reassessment to UAP. Expect **SBAR** with **quantified** vitals, **emesis/stool** description, and **orthostatic** trends when provided. Feeding questions reward **aspiration** prevention and **holding feeds** when ileus or perforation risk rises.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Ostomy Complications and Troubleshooting
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Ostomy Complications and Troubleshooting | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN gastrointestinal review: Ostomy Complications and Troubleshooting — assessment, nursing priorities, red flags, bleeding and obstruction safety, infection control, Canada- and US-friendly clinical judgment.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Ostomy Complications and Troubleshooting** applies to **post-abdominal** and **ostomy** clients: **ileus** vs **obstruction** timelines, **first flatus** and **bowel** return, **wound** and **drain** surveillance, **fever** with **tachycardia** as **leak** or **abscess** red flags, and **stoma** **ischemia**, **retraction**, or **high output** complications. Prioritize **early mobilization** when safe, **DVT** prevention per orders, and **escalation** for **rigid abdomen** or **uncontrolled** pain.

Connect [ostomy care](LESSON:ostomy-skin-protection), [bowel obstruction vs ileus](LESSON:bowel-obstruction-vs-paralytic-ileus), [acute pancreatitis care](LESSON:acute-pancreatitis-nursing-care), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Eliminate answers that **delay IV access** in **unstable bleed**, **offer food** before **NPO** rules are cleared in acute abdomen vignettes, or **delegate** unstable reassessment to UAP. Expect **SBAR** with **quantified** vitals, **emesis/stool** description, and **orthostatic** trends when provided. Post-op stems stack **fever + tachycardia + ileus**—choose assessment and **notify** before routine ambulation alone.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Ileus:** absent **bowel sounds**, **distension**, **NPO** + supportive care; differentiate from **complete** obstruction.
- **Surgical complications:** **fever**, **tachycardia**, **increasing** pain out of proportion → **notify**.
- **Ostomy:** assess **stoma color** (dusky = ischemia), **skin** integrity, **output** volume and consistency.
- **Mobility:** progressive ambulation when orders allow to reduce **ileus** risk.
- **DVT:** prophylaxis per orders unless contraindicated.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** **POD 2** after **colectomy**, the client has **increasing** midline pain, **HR** **124**, **temperature** **38.6 °C**, and **minimal** flatus.

**Fork:** **Peritonitis** until cleared—**notify surgeon/provider**, **prepare** for **imaging** or **return to OR** themes, **avoid** **forcing** **PO** intake; support **IV** fluids and **monitor** **UOP**.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Inspect, listen, palpate** themes only within scope and orders—**integrate** with **vitals** and **labs**.
- **Unstable bleed** or **acute abdomen** → **NPO**, **access**, **monitor**, **notify** before teaching.
- **Synthesis:** GI nursing is often **resuscitation in disguise**—trend **perfusion** and **mentation**.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Infection Control in GI Patients** ties **enteric** pathogens, **hepatitis** transmission routes, and **contact/droplet** precautions to **dehydration** and **electrolyte** loss. sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:

- **Nursing priorities:** airway and **aspiration** protection; **volume** resuscitation per orders; **infection** and **isolation** discipline; **pain** assessment without masking **surgical** catastrophe.
- **Safety / red flags:** **rigid abdomen**, **unexplained** hypotension, **maroon** stools, **coffee-ground** emesis, **confusion** with **liver** failure, **high-output** stoma.
- **Patient teaching (when stable):** diet progression, **medication** adherence, **when to return** for bleeding or fever.
- **Clinical reasoning:** compare **new** vs **baseline**; pick **assess + stabilize + notify** before teaching.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A hospitalized adult with concerns related to **Bowel Sounds and What They Mean** develops **worsening abdominal distension**, **tachycardia**, and **dizziness** when standing. You note **guarding** on light palpation per order.

**Fork:** **NPO**, **IV access**, **repeat vitals** including **orthostatics** if ordered, **prepare labs** per protocol, and **notify the provider** with **SBAR** including **stool/emesis** details—not routine hygiene first.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:

preTest.question: For Bowel Sounds and What They Mean, which action best matches NCLEX-RN prioritization?
options: Finish charting before reassessing a client with hypotension and melena
Establish IV access, monitor vitals and mentation, and notify the provider with objective findings when unstable criteria are met
Offer a regular diet to rule out anxiety-related abdominal pain
Send the UAP alone to evaluate new rigid abdomen and fever
preTest.rationale: GI emergencies require immediate assessment, IV access when indicated, perfusion and bleeding surveillance, and timely provider communication before deferrable tasks.

preTest.question: A client with risk related to Bowel Sounds and What They Mean has active hematemesis and altered consciousness. What is the priority?
options: Complete discharge teaching about diet
Protect airway, position to reduce aspiration risk, call for help, and prepare for urgent interventions per orders
Ambulate the client to stimulate bowel function
Wait for the next scheduled assessment in two hours
preTest.rationale: Active upper GI bleeding with altered consciousness threatens airway and perfusion; nursing prioritizes airway protection, assistance, and urgent escalation.

postTest.question: Which finding most urgently escalates care in a GI context for Bowel Sounds and What They Mean?
options: Mild bloating after lunch in afebrile client

Chronic heartburn unchanged from baseline
postTest.rationale: Peritoneal signs with fever and tachycardia suggest perforation or intra-abdominal catastrophe until evaluated and require immediate escalation.

postTest.question: When is patient teaching about Bowel Sounds and What They Mean most appropriate?
options: During active hemodynamic instability from GI bleeding

Only after discharge without follow-up

postTest.rationale: Education is safest and most effective after acute threats are stabilized and the patient can engage.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Enteral Feeding Tubes (NG, PEG)** covers **enteral** and **parenteral** support: **NG/PEG** placement verification themes, **residual** checks when policy allows, **head-of-bed** elevation, **aspiration** prevention, **slowing or holding** feeds for **distension** or **emesis**, and **TPN** line **asepsis** with **glucose** and **electrolyte** monitoring per orders. Boards punish **continuing tube feeds** when **aspiration** risk is high or **perforation** is suspected.

Link [enteral feeding tube safety](LESSON:enteral-feeding-tube-safety), [acute pancreatitis care](LESSON:acute-pancreatitis-nursing-care), [bowel obstruction vs ileus](LESSON:bowel-obstruction-vs-paralytic-ileus), and [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners use **first**, **priority**, and **most important** language. Feeding questions reward **aspiration** prevention and **holding feeds** when ileus or perforation risk rises.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **NG/PEG:** confirm **placement** per policy before feeding; **HOB** elevated; **flush** per protocol.
- **Aspiration:** **hold** feeds with **emesis**, **high residuals** when ordered thresholds met, **new** hypoxia or **cough** with feeds.
- **TPN:** dedicated **central** line care; **strict** aseptic technique; monitor **glucose**, **electrolytes**, **triglycerides** when shown.
- **Malnutrition:** **BMI**, **unintentional** weight loss, **albumin** themes; refer **dietitian** when stable.
- **Refeeding:** risk awareness in **starved** clients when stem cues electrolytes.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** During **continuous tube feeding**, the client **coughs**, **SpO₂** drops to **89%**, and you note **bilateral crackles** new this shift.

**Fork:** **Stop or hold feeds** per protocol, **elevate HOB**, **suction** as needed, **notify provider**, and **assess** for **aspiration**—not increasing **rate** to “finish the bag.”

sections.heading: Core concept

- **Gastroenteritis:** **oral** rehydration when mild and tolerating; **IV** fluids when **unable** to keep up or hypotensive.
